{{nihongo|'''Wakenosu Shrine'''|和気能須命神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Oki Province]].

name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 和気能須神社 |画像 = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居 |所在地 = {{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|隠岐郡|Q735735}}{{jalink|隠岐の島町|Q1205039}}下西1607{{sfn|島根県神社庁}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 和気能須命{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|式内小社|Q134917287}}、旧{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|pp=494-495}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|春日造|Q3193781}}変態{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}} |別名 = {{jalink|松尾神社|Q11529677}}{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}} |札所等 = |例祭 = 3月17日{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}}{{ref|島根県神社庁}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=和気能須
神社|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''和気能須神社'''（わけのすじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|隠岐郡|Q735735}}{{jalink|隠岐の島町|Q1205039}}下西に所在する{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}（小社）で、{{jalink|旧社格}}は村社。通称は'''松尾神社'''。 === 概要 === 勧請年は不詳だが、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載される「和気能湏命神社」の比定社である{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。『{{jalink|国内神名帳|Q11420490|隠州神名帳}}』には下西の惣社「従一位和気能酒大明神」として記載されている{{sfn|半田|1983|p=979}}{{efn|この「従一位」は{{jalink|宗源宣旨}}と考えられている{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。}}。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}5年（1872年）、村社に列せられる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 「松尾神社」と俗称され、{{jalink|酒造}}の神として知られる{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}{{efn|社地に隣接して流れる谷川が酒造に適した良質な水であったことから「和気能酒」と書かれ、また中古には{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}から{{jalink|松尾大社|Q710302|松尾明神}}を勧請したと伝わる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=979}}。「和気能酒」と表記するようになってから酒造の神とされるようになったとする説もある{{sfn|離火之舎|1987c|p=293}}。}}。神紋は「丸に{{jalink|瓢箪}}」{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}}。 === 祭神 === '''和気能須命'''（わけのすのみこと） {{jalink|水若酢神社|Q11549109}}の水若酢命、{{jalink|玉若酢命神社|Q11572773}}の玉若酢命と関連する神と考えられ、神格は不詳だが人格神とする立場と自然神とする立場に大きく分けられる{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}。 * 人格神説 :この説は清水真三郎による『島根県史』に載せられた論考{{sfn|清水|1923}}に代表される{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|離火之舎|1987b|p=256}}。清水は「水=瑞」と「玉」はそれぞれ美称であり、両若酢命に血縁関係を想定した{{sfn|清水|1923|p=227}}。そこで「1. {{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723|景行}}朝の皇族は諸国に分布して所謂「別」の分治が行はれたのである」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=228-229}}、「2. 景行第五皇子{{jalink|大酢別皇子}}は隠岐「別」として隠岐に分治せられたらしい」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=229-230}}、「3. 和気能須命神社は大酢別命を祭つたものであらう」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=230-232}}と論を進め、和気能須命神社を祖神とする水若酢命・玉若酢命は{{jalink|島後|Q3623017}}を二分して統治したのだと推測した{{sfn|清水|1923|p=231}}。すなわち当社祭神の'''和気能須命を大酢別皇子に比定する'''説である。 * 自然神説 : この説は志賀剛による『式内社の研究』{{sfn|志賀|1981}}に代表される{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}。志賀は和気能須命を「和気能若酢命」の「若」が省略されたものとみなし、玉若酢命神社の摂社格にあたる神社であると考えた{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=558}}。神格としては、'''当社の傍にある池の水を湧かせる神と推定した'''{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=558}}。なお玉若酢命は玉若酢命神社の下西村の「地下水は島内一の清水である」ことから「玉水湧かす」の意味{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=557}}、水若酢命は水若酢神社の旧社地である山田を流れる山田川と「若返りの水」を意味する「{{jalink|穏地郡|Q11597172|越知}}（おち）」の地名に注目してやはり清水を湧かせる「水ワカス命」であるとして{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=561}}、玉若酢命神社と水若酢神社は対称的であるとしつつもこれら3社の祭神同士の直接の関係を考えなかった{{sfn|志賀|pp=557,560}}。 === 施設 === ;本殿 :{{jalink|春日造|Q3193781}}変態{{efn|「{{jalink|隠岐造|Q11659055}}」とする文献もある{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。}}で、間口1間・奥行1.3間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。本殿の軸立は{{jalink|寛政|Q1195131}}2年（1790年）と考えられる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 ;通殿 :{{jalink|切妻造|Q13308575}}で{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}、間口1.3間・奥行2.3間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。 ;拝殿 :{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}}で{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}、間口2.1間・奥行2.1間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。 ;参篭所{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}} :隠岐では、産土神社に病気回復、安産、大漁、入学などの祈願をした後、成就のお礼として神社境内に存在する「コモシャ」「コモリヤ」と呼ばれる小屋に一晩籠る「オコモリ」という行事が存在した{{sfn|大津|1973|p=310}}。 ;鳥居 :明神鳥居{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 === 脚注 === === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist|20em}} === 参考文献 === * {{cite book|和書|author=大津武久|chapter=神仏祭祀|editor=石塚尊俊|title=隠岐島の民俗：隠岐島民俗資料緊急調査報告|publisher=島根県神社庁|year=1973|pages=285-327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}} |ref={{sfnref|大津|1973}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=志賀剛|title=式内社の研究 第4巻|publisher=雄山閣|year=1981|id={{JP番号|81037945}} |ref={{sfnref|志賀|1981}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=清水真三郎|chapter=仲哀朝以後反正朝に於ける隠岐と出雲|editor=島根県学務部島根県史編纂掛|title=島根県史 三|publisher=島根県|year=1923|pages=223-260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|清水|1923}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |editor1=高橋忠 |editor2=水師重吉 |editor3=伊藤征男 |chapter=和気能須神社 |title=神国島根 |edition=復刻版 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |year=1996 |pages=494-495 |ref={{sfnref|高橋他編|1996}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=半田弥一郎 |chapter=12 和気能湏命神社 |editor=式内社研究会 |title=式内社調査報告第21巻 山陰道4 |publisher=皇学館大学出版部 |year=1983 |pages=977-980 |id={{JP番号|83032918}} |ref={{sfnref|半田|1983}} }} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=離火之舎 |chapter=451. 玉若酢命 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98 |title=島根の神々 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |id={{JP番号|88012198}} |year=1987 |pages=183-184 |ref={{sfnref|離火之舎|1987a}} }}離火之舎 1987a。 * {{Cite book |和書 |author=離火之舎 |chapter=610. 水若酢命 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135 |title=島根の神々 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |id={{JP番号|88012198}} |year=1987 |pages=256-257 |ref={{sfnref|離火之舎|1987b}} }}離火之舎 1987b。 * {{Cite book |和書 |author=離火之舎 |chapter=685. 和気能須命 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153 |title=島根の神々 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |id={{JP番号|88012198}} |year=1987 |page=293 |ref={{sfnref|離火之舎|1987c}} }}離火之舎 1987c。 * {{cite web|title=和気能須神社 - 神社詳細|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=島根県神社庁|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|島根県神社庁}}}} === 関連項目 name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 和気能須神社 |画像 = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居 |所在地 = {{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|隠岐郡|Q735735}}{{jalink|隠岐の島町|Q1205039}}下西1607{{sfn|島根県神社庁}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 和気能須命{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|式内小社|Q134917287}}、旧{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|pp=494-495}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|春日造|Q3193781}}変態{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}} |別名 = {{jalink|松尾神社|Q11529677}}{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}} |札所等 = |例祭 = 3月17日{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}}{{ref|島根県神社庁}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=和気能須
神社|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''和気能須神社'''（わけのすじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|隠岐郡|Q735735}}{{jalink|隠岐の島町|Q1205039}}下西に所在する{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}（小社）で、{{jalink|旧社格}}は村社。通称は'''松尾神社'''。 === 概要 === 勧請年は不詳だが、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載される「和気能湏命神社」の比定社である{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。『{{jalink|国内神名帳|Q11420490|隠州神名帳}}』には下西の惣社「従一位和気能酒大明神」として記載されている{{sfn|半田|1983|p=979}}{{efn|この「従一位」は{{jalink|宗源宣旨}}と考えられている{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。}}。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}5年（1872年）、村社に列せられる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 「松尾神社」と俗称され、{{jalink|酒造}}の神として知られる{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}{{efn|社地に隣接して流れる谷川が酒造に適した良質な水であったことから「和気能酒」と書かれ、また中古には{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}から{{jalink|松尾大社|Q710302|松尾明神}}を勧請したと伝わる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=979}}。「和気能酒」と表記するようになってから酒造の神とされるようになったとする説もある{{sfn|離火之舎|1987c|p=293}}。}}。神紋は「丸に{{jalink|瓢箪}}」{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}}。 === 祭神 === '''和気能須命'''（わけのすのみこと） {{jalink|水若酢神社|Q11549109}}の水若酢命、{{jalink|玉若酢命神社|Q11572773}}の玉若酢命と関連する神と考えられ、神格は不詳だが人格神とする立場と自然神とする立場に大きく分けられる{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}。 * 人格神説 :この説は清水真三郎による『島根県史』に載せられた論考{{sfn|清水|1923}}に代表される{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|離火之舎|1987b|p=256}}。清水は「水=瑞」と「玉」はそれぞれ美称であり、両若酢命に血縁関係を想定した{{sfn|清水|1923|p=227}}。そこで「1. {{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723|景行}}朝の皇族は諸国に分布して所謂「別」の分治が行はれたのである」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=228-229}}、「2. 景行第五皇子{{jalink|大酢別皇子}}は隠岐「別」として隠岐に分治せられたらしい」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=229-230}}、「3. 和気能須命神社は大酢別命を祭つたものであらう」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=230-232}}と論を進め、和気能須命神社を祖神とする水若酢命・玉若酢命は{{jalink|島後|Q3623017}}を二分して統治したのだと推測した{{sfn|清水|1923|p=231}}。すなわち当社祭神の'''和気能須命を大酢別皇子に比定する'''説である。 * 自然神説 : この説は志賀剛による『式内社の研究』{{sfn|志賀|1981}}に代表される{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}。志賀は和気能須命を「和気能若酢命」の「若」が省略されたものとみなし、玉若酢命神社の摂社格にあたる神社であると考えた{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=558}}。神格としては、'''当社の傍にある池の水を湧かせる神と推定した'''{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=558}}。なお玉若酢命は玉若酢命神社の下西村の「地下水は島内一の清水である」ことから「玉水湧かす」の意味{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=557}}、水若酢命は水若酢神社の旧社地である山田を流れる山田川と「若返りの水」を意味する「{{jalink|穏地郡|Q11597172|越知}}（おち）」の地名に注目してやはり清水を湧かせる「水ワカス命」であるとして{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=561}}、玉若酢命神社と水若酢神社は対称的であるとしつつもこれら3社の祭神同士の直接の関係を考えなかった{{sfn|志賀|pp=557,560}}。 === 施設 === ;本殿 :{{jalink|春日造|Q3193781}}変態{{efn|「{{jalink|隠岐造|Q11659055}}」とする文献もある{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。}}で、間口1間・奥行1.3間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。本殿の軸立は{{jalink|寛政|Q1195131}}2年（1790年）と考えられる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 ;通殿 :{{jalink|切妻造|Q13308575}}で{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}、間口1.3間・奥行2.3間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。 ;拝殿 :{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}}で{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}、間口2.1間・奥行2.1間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。 ;参篭所{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}} :隠岐では、産土神社に病気回復、安産、大漁、入学などの祈願をした後、成就のお礼として神社境内に存在する「コモシャ」「コモリヤ」と呼ばれる小屋に一晩籠る「オコモリ」という行事が存在した{{sfn|大津|1973|p=310}}。 ;鳥居 :明神鳥居{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 === 脚注 === === 注釈 === |ref={{sfnref|大津|1973}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=志賀剛|title=式内社の研究 第4巻|publisher=雄山閣|year=1981|id={{JP番号|81037945}} |ref={{sfnref|志賀|1981}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=清水真三郎|chapter=仲哀朝以後反正朝に於ける隠岐と出雲|editor=島根県学務部島根県史編纂掛|title=島根県史 三|publisher=島根県|year=1923|pages=223-260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|清水|1923}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |editor1=高橋忠 |editor2=水師重吉 |editor3=伊藤征男 |chapter=和気能須神社 |title=神国島根 |edition=復刻版 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |year=1996 |pages=494-495 |ref={{sfnref|高橋他編|1996}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=半田弥一郎 |chapter=12 和気能湏命神社 |editor=式内社研究会 |title=式内社調査報告第21巻 山陰道4 |publisher=皇学館大学出版部 |year=1983 |pages=977-980 |id={{JP番号|83032918}} |ref={{sfnref|半田|1983}} }} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=離火之舎 |chapter=451. 玉若酢命 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98 |title=島根の神々 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |id={{JP番号|88012198}} |year=1987 |pages=183-184 |ref={{sfnref|離火之舎|1987a}} }}離火之舎 1987a。 * {{Cite book |和書 |author=離火之舎 name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 和気能須神社 |画像 = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居 |所在地 = {{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|隠岐郡|Q735735}}{{jalink|隠岐の島町|Q1205039}}下西1607{{sfn|島根県神社庁}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 和気能須命{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|式内小社|Q134917287}}、旧{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|pp=494-495}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{jalink|春日造|Q3193781}}変態{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}} |別名 = {{jalink|松尾神社|Q11529677}}{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}} |札所等 = |例祭 = 3月17日{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}}{{ref|島根県神社庁}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=和気能須
神社|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''和気能須神社'''（わけのすじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|島根県|Q132751}}{{jalink|隠岐郡|Q735735}}{{jalink|隠岐の島町|Q1205039}}下西に所在する{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}（小社）で、{{jalink|旧社格}}は村社。通称は'''松尾神社'''。 === 概要 === 勧請年は不詳だが、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載される「和気能湏命神社」の比定社である{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。『{{jalink|国内神名帳|Q11420490|隠州神名帳}}』には下西の惣社「従一位和気能酒大明神」として記載されている{{sfn|半田|1983|p=979}}{{efn|この「従一位」は{{jalink|宗源宣旨}}と考えられている{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。}}。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}5年（1872年）、村社に列せられる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 「松尾神社」と俗称され、{{jalink|酒造}}の神として知られる{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}{{efn|社地に隣接して流れる谷川が酒造に適した良質な水であったことから「和気能酒」と書かれ、また中古には{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}から{{jalink|松尾大社|Q710302|松尾明神}}を勧請したと伝わる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=979}}。「和気能酒」と表記するようになってから酒造の神とされるようになったとする説もある{{sfn|離火之舎|1987c|p=293}}。}}。神紋は「丸に{{jalink|瓢箪}}」{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=494}}。 === 祭神 === '''和気能須命'''（わけのすのみこと） {{jalink|水若酢神社|Q11549109}}の水若酢命、{{jalink|玉若酢命神社|Q11572773}}の玉若酢命と関連する神と考えられ、神格は不詳だが人格神とする立場と自然神とする立場に大きく分けられる{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}。 * 人格神説 :この説は清水真三郎による『島根県史』に載せられた論考{{sfn|清水|1923}}に代表される{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|離火之舎|1987b|p=256}}。清水は「水=瑞」と「玉」はそれぞれ美称であり、両若酢命に血縁関係を想定した{{sfn|清水|1923|p=227}}。そこで「1. {{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723|景行}}朝の皇族は諸国に分布して所謂「別」の分治が行はれたのである」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=228-229}}、「2. 景行第五皇子{{jalink|大酢別皇子}}は隠岐「別」として隠岐に分治せられたらしい」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=229-230}}、「3. 和気能須命神社は大酢別命を祭つたものであらう」{{sfn|清水|1923|pp=230-232}}と論を進め、和気能須命神社を祖神とする水若酢命・玉若酢命は{{jalink|島後|Q3623017}}を二分して統治したのだと推測した{{sfn|清水|1923|p=231}}。すなわち当社祭神の'''和気能須命を大酢別皇子に比定する'''説である。 * 自然神説 : この説は志賀剛による『式内社の研究』{{sfn|志賀|1981}}に代表される{{sfn|離火之舎|1987a|p=184}}。志賀は和気能須命を「和気能若酢命」の「若」が省略されたものとみなし、玉若酢命神社の摂社格にあたる神社であると考えた{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=558}}。神格としては、'''当社の傍にある池の水を湧かせる神と推定した'''{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=558}}。なお玉若酢命は玉若酢命神社の下西村の「地下水は島内一の清水である」ことから「玉水湧かす」の意味{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=557}}、水若酢命は水若酢神社の旧社地である山田を流れる山田川と「若返りの水」を意味する「{{jalink|穏地郡|Q11597172|越知}}（おち）」の地名に注目してやはり清水を湧かせる「水ワカス命」であるとして{{sfn|志賀|1981|p=561}}、玉若酢命神社と水若酢神社は対称的であるとしつつもこれら3社の祭神同士の直接の関係を考えなかった{{sfn|志賀|pp=557,560}}。 === 施設 === ;本殿 :{{jalink|春日造|Q3193781}}変態{{efn|「{{jalink|隠岐造|Q11659055}}」とする文献もある{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。}}で、間口1間・奥行1.3間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。本殿の軸立は{{jalink|寛政|Q1195131}}2年（1790年）と考えられる{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 ;通殿 :{{jalink|切妻造|Q13308575}}で{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}、間口1.3間・奥行2.3間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。 ;拝殿 :{{jalink|入母屋造|Q855025}}で{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}、間口2.1間・奥行2.1間{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}}。 ;参篭所{{sfn|高橋他編|1996|p=495}} :隠岐では、産土神社に病気回復、安産、大漁、入学などの祈願をした後、成就のお礼として神社境内に存在する「コモシャ」「コモリヤ」と呼ばれる小屋に一晩籠る「オコモリ」という行事が存在した{{sfn|大津|1973|p=310}}。 ;鳥居 :明神鳥居{{sfn|半田|1983|p=980}}。 === 脚注 === === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist|20em}} === 参考文献 === * {{cite book|和書|author=大津武久|chapter=神仏祭祀|editor=石塚尊俊|title=隠岐島の民俗：隠岐島民俗資料緊急調査報告|publisher=島根県神社庁|year=1973|pages=285-327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}} |ref={{sfnref|大津|1973}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=志賀剛|title=式内社の研究 第4巻|publisher=雄山閣|year=1981|id={{JP番号|81037945}} |ref={{sfnref|志賀|1981}}}} * {{cite book|和書|author=清水真三郎|chapter=仲哀朝以後反正朝に於ける隠岐と出雲|editor=島根県学務部島根県史編纂掛|title=島根県史 三|publisher=島根県|year=1923|pages=223-260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|清水|1923}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |editor1=高橋忠 |editor2=水師重吉 |editor3=伊藤征男 |chapter=和気能須神社 |title=神国島根 |edition=復刻版 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |year=1996 |pages=494-495 |ref={{sfnref|高橋他編|1996}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=半田弥一郎 玉若酢命 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98 |title=島根の神々 |publisher=島根県神社庁 |id={{JP番号|88012198}} |year=1987 |pages=183-184 |ref={{sfnref|離火之舎|1987a}} }}離火之舎 1987a。 * {{Cite book |和書 |author=離火之舎 name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{jalink|Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Shimonishi, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Oshu Jinmyōchō}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{jalink|sake brewing}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|Matsuo Myōjin}} was invited from {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Keikō|Q329723|Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{jalink|Ōsu-wake no Ōji}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{jalink|Dōgo Island|Q3623017}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{jalink|Ochi District|Q11597172|Ochi}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{jalink|Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=12 Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine|editor=Society for the Study of Shikinaisha|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San’indō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=183–184|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987a. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=610. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakenosu Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:need translation]]

name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{jalink|Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Shimonishi, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Oshu Jinmyōchō}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{jalink|sake brewing}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|Matsuo Myōjin}} was invited from {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Keikō|Q329723|Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{jalink|Ōsu-wake no Ōji}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{jalink|Dōgo Island|Q3623017}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{jalink|Ochi District|Q11597172|Ochi}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{jalink|Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=12 Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine|editor=Society for the Study of Shikinaisha|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San’indō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=183–184|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987a. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=610. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakenosu Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]]

name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{jalink|Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Shimonishi, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Oshu Jinmyōchō}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{jalink|sake brewing}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|Matsuo Myōjin}} was invited from {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Keikō|Q329723|Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{jalink|Ōsu-wake no Ōji}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{jalink|Dōgo Island|Q3623017}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{jalink|Ochi District|Q11597172|Ochi}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{jalink|Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=12 Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine|editor=Society for the Study of Shikinaisha|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San’indō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=183–184|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987a. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=610. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakenosu Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]]

name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{jalink|Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Shimonishi, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Oshu Jinmyōchō}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{jalink|sake brewing}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|Matsuo Myōjin}} was invited from {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Keikō|Q329723|Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{jalink|Ōsu-wake no Ōji}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{jalink|Dōgo Island|Q3623017}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{jalink|Ochi District|Q11597172|Ochi}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{jalink|Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{jalink|Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Shimonishi, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Oshu Jinmyōchō}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{jalink|sake brewing}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|Matsuo Myōjin}} was invited from {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{jalink|Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Keikō|Q329723|Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{jalink|Ōsu-wake no Ōji}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{jalink|Dōgo Island|Q3623017}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{jalink|Ochi District|Q11597172|Ochi}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{jalink|Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{jalink|Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735}}{{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}}, former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Shimonishi, {{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Oshu Jinmyōchō}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{ill||lt=Sōgen Senji|UNKNOWN}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{ill||lt=sake brewing|UNKNOWN}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Myōjin}} was invited from {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109|WD=Q11549109}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773|WD=Q11572773}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|lt=Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{ill||lt=Ōsu-wake no Ōji|UNKNOWN}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{ill|lt=Dōgo Island|Q3623017|WD=Q3623017}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{ill|lt=Ochi District|Q11597172|WD=Q11597172|lt=Ochi}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{ill|lt=Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=12 Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine|editor=Society for the Study of Shikinaisha|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San’indō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=183–184|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987a. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=610. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakenosu Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]]
