content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Shimonishi, {{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Oshu Jinmyōchō|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōgen Senji|1=UNKNOWN}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sake brewing|1=UNKNOWN}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Myōjin|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} was invited from {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109|WD=Q11549109|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773|WD=Q11572773|1=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|lt=Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsu-wake no Ōji|1=UNKNOWN}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{ill|lt=Dōgo Island|Q3623017|WD=Q3623017|1=Dōgojima}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{ill|lt=Ochi District|Q11597172|WD=Q11597172|lt=Ochi|1=Ochi district}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{ill|lt=Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=12 Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine|editor=Society for the Study of Shikinaisha|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San’indō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=183–184|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987a. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=610. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Shimonishi, {{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Oshu Jinmyōchō|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōgen Senji|1=UNKNOWN}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sake brewing|1=UNKNOWN}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Myōjin|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} was invited from {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109|WD=Q11549109|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773|WD=Q11572773|1=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|lt=Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsu-wake no Ōji|1=UNKNOWN}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{ill|lt=Dōgo Island|Q3623017|WD=Q3623017|1=Dōgojima}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{ill|lt=Ochi District|Q11597172|WD=Q11597172|lt=Ochi|1=Ochi district}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{ill|lt=Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Taketoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report of Oki Island Folklore Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=12 Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine|editor=Society for the Study of Shikinaisha|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha, Vol. 21, San’indō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/98|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=183–184|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987a. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=610. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Shimonishi, {{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Oshu Jinmyōchō|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōgen Senji|1=UNKNOWN}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sake brewing|1=UNKNOWN}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Myōjin|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} was invited from {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109|WD=Q11549109|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773|WD=Q11572773|1=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|lt=Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsu-wake no Ōji|1=UNKNOWN}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{ill|lt=Dōgo Island|Q3623017|WD=Q3623017|1=Dōgojima}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{ill|lt=Ochi District|Q11597172|WD=Q11597172|lt=Ochi|1=Ochi district}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{ill|lt=Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane Prefecture|year=1923|pages=223–260|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3438565/1/143|ref={{sfnref|Shimizu|1923}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|editor1=Tadashi Takahashi|editor2=Shigeyoshi Mizushi|editor3=Ikuo Itō|chapter=Wakenosu Shrine|title=Shinkoku Shimane|edition=Reprint|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1996|pages=494–495|ref={{sfnref|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996}}}} * {{cite University Press|year=1983|pages=977–980|id={{JP Number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=451. Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/135|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=256–257|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987b. * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Rikabi-no-ya|chapter=685. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/153|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|id={{JP Number|88012198}}|year=1987|page=293|ref={{sfnref|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c}}}} Rikabi-no-ya 1987c. * {{cite web|title=Wakenosu Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/5bda011cbb4d229a5ca05cf0f9779a0f4635ce35.html|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|accessdate=2025-09-16|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}}}} === Related Articles === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Wakenosu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:Municipal History of Okinoshima]]

[[ja:和気能須神社]] {{Expand Japanese|和気能須神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakenosu Shrine | native_name = 和気能須命神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Wakenosu Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.206769|133.307311}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Wakenosu Shrine'''|和気能須命神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. == name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Shimonishi, {{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Oshu Jinmyōchō|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōgen Senji|1=UNKNOWN}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sake brewing|1=UNKNOWN}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Myōjin|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} was invited from {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109|WD=Q11549109|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773|WD=Q11572773|1=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|lt=Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsu-wake no Ōji|1=UNKNOWN}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{ill|lt=Dōgo Island|Q3623017|WD=Q3623017|1=Dōgojima}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{ill|lt=Ochi District|Q11597172|WD=Q11597172|lt=Ochi|1=Ochi district}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{ill|lt=Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Tsuyoshi Shiga|title=Studies on Shikinaisha, Vol. 4|publisher=Yūzankaku|year=1981|id={{JP Number|81037945}}|ref={{sfnref|Shiga|1981}}}} * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Shinzaburō Shimizu|chapter=Oki and Izumo from the Reign of Emperor Chūai through Emperor Hanzei|editor=Shimane Prefectural Academic Affairs Department, Shimane Prefectural History Compilation Office|title=Shimane Prefectural History, Vol. 3|publisher=Shimane == name in kana (P1814) == * ワケノスノ-Engishiki Jinmyōchō * わけのすじんじゃ == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町下西', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:和気能須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041143}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Wakenosu Shrine |Image = [[File:Wakenosu Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}} Shimonishi 1607{{sfn|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deity = Wakenosu-no-Mikoto{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Sacred Object = |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|pp=494–495}} |Founded = |Main Hall = Modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} |Other Names = {{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}}{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}} |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = March 17{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}{{ref|Shimane Prefectural Shrine Association}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Wakenosu
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Wakenosu Shrine''' (わけのすじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Shimonishi, {{ill|lt=Okinoshima Town|Q1205039|WD=Q1205039|1=Okinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. Its common name is '''Matsuo Shrine'''. === Overview === The year of enshrinement is unknown, but it is identified as the shrine corresponding to “Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine” listed in the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}*{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Oshu Jinmyōchō|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}*, it is recorded as the collective shrine of Shimonishi under the name “Junior First Rank Wakenosu Ōmyōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}{{efn|This “Junior First Rank” is thought to refer to a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōgen Senji|1=UNKNOWN}} decree{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 (1872), it was ranked as a village shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. Commonly called “Matsuo Shrine,” it is known as a deity of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sake brewing|1=UNKNOWN}} {{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}{{efn|Because the mountain stream flowing beside the shrine precincts provided high-quality water suitable for brewing sake, the name was written as “Wakenosake.” It is also said that in the medieval period, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Myōjin|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} was invited from {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=979}}. There is also a theory that it came to be regarded as a deity of sake brewing only after the name began to be written as “Wakenosake”{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987c|p=293}}.}}. The shrine crest is a “gourd within a circle”{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=494}}. === Enshrined Deity === '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto''' (わけのすのみこと) This deity is thought to be related to Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|Q11549109|WD=Q11549109|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine|Q11572773|WD=Q11572773|1=Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine}}. While the divine nature is unclear, there are two major views: one that considers the deity a personal god, and another that sees it as a nature god{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. * Personal Deity Theory : This view is typified by an essay included in *Shimane Prefectural History* by Shimizu Shinzaburō{{sfn|Shimizu|1923}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987b|p=256}}. Shimizu regarded “mizu (water) = mizu (auspicious)” and “tama (jewel)” as honorific expressions, and assumed a blood relationship between the two Wakasu deities{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=227}}. He then argued: “1. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|lt=Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}, imperial relatives were distributed across various provinces and so-called ‘branch’ administrations were established”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=228–229}}; “2. The fifth imperial prince of Keikō, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsu-wake no Ōji|1=UNKNOWN}}, seems to have been assigned to Oki as a ‘branch’ and governed there”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=229–230}}; “3. Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine likely enshrines Ōsu-wake-no-Mikoto”{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|pp=230–232}}. From this, he inferred that Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto and Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto, whose ancestral shrine was Wakenosu-no-Mikoto Shrine, divided and governed {{ill|lt=Dōgo Island|Q3623017|WD=Q3623017|1=Dōgojima}}{{sfn|Shimizu|1923|p=231}}. In other words, this theory identifies the enshrined deity '''Wakenosu-no-Mikoto with Prince Ōsu-wake'''. * Nature Deity Theory : This view is represented by Shiga Tsuyoshi’s *Studies on Shikinaisha*{{sfn|Shiga|1981}}{{sfn|Rikabi-no-ya|1987a|p=184}}. Shiga considered Wakenosu-no-Mikoto to be a shortened form of “Wakenowakasu-no-Mikoto,” with the “waka” omitted, and regarded the shrine as corresponding to a subsidiary shrine of Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. As for the divine nature, he inferred it to be '''a deity who causes the water of a pond beside the shrine to spring forth'''{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=558}}. He further interpreted Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto as meaning “causing jeweled water to spring forth,” based on the exceptionally pure groundwater of Shimonishi Village where Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine is located{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=557}}; and Mizuwakasu-no-Mikoto as a deity who causes pure water to spring forth (“Mizu-wakasu-no-Mikoto”), focusing on the Yamada River flowing through Yamada, the former shrine site of Mizuwakasu Shrine, and the place name {{ill|lt=Ochi District|Q11597172|WD=Q11597172|lt=Ochi|1=Ochi district}} meaning “rejuvenating water”{{sfn|Shiga|1981|p=561}}. While he considered Tamawakasu-no-Mikoto Shrine and Mizuwakasu Shrine to be symmetrical, he did not posit a direct relationship among the enshrined deities of these three shrines{{sfn|Shiga|pp=557, 560}}. === Facilities === ;Main Hall : A modified {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{efn|Some sources describe it as {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}.}}, measuring 1 ken in width and 1.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. The erection of the main hall’s pillars is thought to date to {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 2 (1790){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. ;Connecting Hall : {{ill|lt=Kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 1.3 ken in width and 2.3 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Worship Hall : {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}, measuring 2.1 ken in width and 2.1 ken in depth{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}}. ;Pilgrims’ Lodging{{sfn|Takahashi et al., eds.|1996|p=495}} : In Oki, there was a custom called “okomori,” in which people would pray at their tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, abundant catches, or school admission, and then, as thanks for fulfillment, spend a night secluded in a hut called “komosha” or “komoriya” located within the shrine precincts{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ;Torii : Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=980}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Bibliography === * {{cite
